result.markdown:
--- title: "Contrasts" subtitle: "Create named hypotheses from design-matrix columns." jupyter: python3 execute: freeze: auto --- ## A 2 x 2 Event Design The original R vignette built contrasts for a factorial event model. In Python, start by making the factorial cells explicit in the event table. ::: {#c72b7d9a .cell execution_count=1} ``` {.python .cell-code} import numpy as np import pandas as pd import fmrimod as fm from fmrimod.design_colmap import design_colmap design = pd.DataFrame({ "category": ["face", "face", "scene", "scene"] * 3, "attention": ["attend", "ignore", "attend", "ignore"] * 3, "onset": np.arange(0, 96, 8), }) sf = fm.SamplingFrame(blocklens=120, TR=1.0) model = fm.event_model( 'onset ~ hrf(category, attention, basis="spmg1")', data=design, sampling_frame=sf, ) design_colmap(model)[["col", "name", "condition"]] ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=1} ```{=html}
col name condition
0 1 category_category.face category.face
1 2 category_category.scene category.scene
``` ::: ::: ## Pair and Unit Contrasts A pair contrast is just a named vector in design-column order. This explicit representation is often the clearest way to audit the hypothesis before fitting a model. ::: {#96e1755f .cell execution_count=2} ``` {.python .cell-code} columns = model.column_names face_cols = [i for i, name in enumerate(columns) if "face" in name] scene_cols = [i for i, name in enumerate(columns) if "scene" in name] face_vs_scene = np.zeros(len(columns)) face_vs_scene[face_cols] = 1 / len(face_cols) face_vs_scene[scene_cols] = -1 / len(scene_cols) pd.DataFrame({"column": columns, "weight": face_vs_scene}) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=2} ```{=html}
column weight
0 category_category.face 1.0
1 category_category.scene -1.0
``` ::: ::: A unit-style contrast compares a group of columns to the implicit baseline. ::: {#996784f6 .cell execution_count=3} ``` {.python .cell-code} face_vs_baseline = np.zeros(len(columns)) face_vs_baseline[face_cols] = 1 / len(face_cols) pd.DataFrame({"column": columns, "weight": face_vs_baseline}) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=3} ```{=html}
column weight
0 category_category.face 1.0
1 category_category.scene 0.0
``` ::: ::: ## Main Effects and Interactions `fmrimod` also exposes fast factorial contrast generators for balanced cell layouts. ::: {#1ce937a0 .cell execution_count=4} ``` {.python .cell-code} cells = pd.DataFrame({ "category": pd.Categorical(["face", "scene"]), "attention": pd.Categorical(["attend", "ignore"]), }) category_main = fm.generate_main_effect_contrast(cells, "category") interaction = fm.generate_interaction_contrast(cells, ["category", "attention"]) category_main.shape, interaction.shape ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=4} ``` ((4, 1), (4, 1)) ``` ::: ::: ::: {#0d0856be .cell execution_count=5} ``` {.python .cell-code} pd.DataFrame( np.column_stack([category_main, interaction]), columns=["category_main", "category_attention_interaction"], ) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=5} ```{=html}
category_main category_attention_interaction
0 1.0 1.0
1 1.0 -1.0
2 -1.0 -1.0
3 -1.0 1.0
``` ::: ::: ## Apply a Contrast After Fitting The GLM helper returns coefficient, standard error, and t-statistic matrices. A contrast estimate can be formed by multiplying the fitted beta matrix by the contrast vector. ::: {#213fcc5a .cell execution_count=6} ``` {.python .cell-code} rng = np.random.default_rng(1) X = np.asarray(model.design_matrix) true_beta = np.linspace(0.4, 1.0, X.shape[1])[:, None] Y = X @ true_beta + rng.normal(scale=0.05, size=(X.shape[0], 4)) fit = fm.fmri_ols_fit(Y, X) contrast_estimate = face_vs_scene @ fit["beta"] contrast_estimate.round(3) ``` ::: {.cell-output .cell-output-display execution_count=6} ``` array([-0.61 , -0.611, -0.593, -0.584]) ``` ::: ::: ## Next - [Linear model](linear-model.qmd) - [Design matrices](design-matrices.qmd)
